skill.name: nodejs-backend-patterns
skill.description: Build production-ready Node.js backend services with Express, Fastify, authentication, middleware, error handling, database access, caching, and API response patterns.
skill.summary: Create Node.js backend services with practical Express, Fastify, authentication, database, and middleware patterns.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found the skill is a Markdown guidance file with TypeScript examples rather than runnable scripts. Most detections are false positives from code fences, relative imports, environment-variable configuration, and resource links. One backend example teaches dynamic SQL identifier construction from object keys, so the skill should publish with a security warning.

medium_findings.title: Unsafe Dynamic SQL Update Example
medium_findings.description: The repository update example builds a SQL SET clause from Object.keys(updates) and interpolates each field name into the query. Values are parameterized, but column names are not allowlisted, so copying this pattern can create SQL injection risk if request bodies are mapped into updates.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The vulnerable pattern is visible in the example: object keys become SQL identifiers before query execution. It is documentation rather than active code, so the risk is unsafe guidance rather than direct exploitation of this skill.

low_findings.title: Static Findings Are Mostly Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The external command, filesystem traversal, prototype manipulation, network URL, credential access, and weak-crypto detections occur inside Markdown examples or ordinary documentation. Examples include TypeScript code fences, relative imports, Object.setPrototypeOf for Error subclassing, environment-variable configuration, and links to public framework documentation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are clearly Markdown documentation and TypeScript examples. I found no executable installer, script file, hidden network call, prompt injection text, or instruction to exfiltrate secrets.

low_findings.title: Broad Network Binding Example Requires Deployment Review
low_findings.description: The Fastify example binds to 0.0.0.0. This is common in containers but exposes the service on all interfaces if copied into a reachable host without firewall or ingress controls.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicit and can be risky in some deployments. It is also legitimate for containerized services, so severity is low in this documentation context.

dangerous_patterns.title: SQL Identifier Interpolation
dangerous_patterns.description: The update example creates setClause by interpolating field names into SQL. Backend examples should validate field names against an allowlist before constructing SQL identifiers.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The query values use placeholders, but the generated column names do not. This is a known unsafe pattern when keys can originate from user-controlled input.

content.value_statement: Teams need repeatable Node.js backend patterns that cover routing, security, data access, and operations. This skill provides practical Express and Fastify examples for building maintainable APIs and services.
actual_capabilities: Shows Express and Fastify server setup with middleware registration.
Explains layered controller, service, repository, and dependency injection patterns.
Provides authentication, authorization, validation, and rate limiting examples.
Covers PostgreSQL, MongoDB, Redis caching, and transaction patterns.
Includes global error handling, API response formatting, and graceful shutdown guidance.
Lists backend best practices for TypeScript services and production APIs.
limitations: It is a guidance-only Markdown skill, not a runnable backend generator.
Examples need adaptation for each project, framework version, and security policy.
The SQL update example needs a field allowlist before production use.
It does not include complete test suites, deployment manifests, or infrastructure code.

use_cases.title: Start a REST API
use_cases.description: Create an Express or Fastify service layout with middleware, controllers, services, repositories, and consistent responses.

use_cases.title: Harden Authentication Flows
use_cases.description: Add JWT authentication, role checks, rate limits, validation, and production-safe error handling to a Node.js API.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Patterns
use_cases.description: Use shared Node.js patterns for database access, dependency injection, caching, transactions, and operational readiness.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic API
prompt_templates.prompt: Use nodejs-backend-patterns to design a small Express API with routes, middleware, validation, and a consistent response format.

prompt_templates.title: Add Service Layers
prompt_templates.prompt: Use nodejs-backend-patterns to refactor my Node.js API into controller, service, repository, and dependency injection layers.

prompt_templates.title: Secure an Auth Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use nodejs-backend-patterns to implement JWT login, refresh tokens, role authorization, rate limiting, and production error handling.

prompt_templates.title: Review Production Readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Use nodejs-backend-patterns to audit my backend design for database pooling, transactions, caching, graceful shutdown, observability, and API consistency.

output_examples.input: Design a maintainable user API for a TypeScript Node.js service.
output_examples.output: A layered plan with controller responsibilities, service methods, repository boundaries, validation middleware, error handling, and response conventions.

output_examples.input: Add authentication and rate limits to an existing Express app.
output_examples.output: A security implementation outline covering token verification, role checks, stricter auth endpoint limits, environment-based secrets, and safe error responses.

output_examples.input: Improve database and cache handling for a growing backend.
output_examples.output: Recommendations for connection pooling, transaction boundaries, Redis cache keys, invalidation strategy, shutdown behavior, and monitoring concerns.

best_practices: Validate all request bodies, query strings, route parameters, and update fields before database access.
Keep controllers thin and place business logic in services with clear repository boundaries.
Use environment variables for configuration, but never log secrets or commit local environment files.
anti_patterns: Do not copy dynamic SQL identifier construction without a strict field allowlist.
Do not expose broad CORS or network bindings without deployment-specific access controls.
Do not return stack traces or raw internal errors from production APIs.

faq.question: Does this skill generate a complete backend application?
faq.answer: No. It provides patterns and examples that you adapt inside an existing or new Node.js project.

faq.question: Which frameworks does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers Express and Fastify, with examples for middleware, routing, validation, authentication, and error handling.

faq.question: Can I use it with TypeScript?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples are written for TypeScript and include typed request, response, service, and repository patterns.

faq.question: Does it include database guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes PostgreSQL pooling, MongoDB connection setup, transaction handling, and Redis caching patterns.

faq.question: Is every example production ready as written?
faq.answer: No. Review each example for your security requirements, especially dynamic SQL updates and deployment exposure.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.
